critic_raw:
Section 3 contains a stated leakage that the design presents as an acceptable tradeoff: 'Fit all transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation) on the combined training and validation data (months 1–12) to stabilize parameter estimates.' This is unusual and problematic—fitting on training+validation combined means that the validation set's distribution is incorporated into the scaling applied to training features. The stated rationale is stability, but this is precisely backwards: scalers fitted on combined data produce validation performance estimates that reflect an optimization over the validation distribution, which makes validation performance an overestimate of test performance on new data. This is a weaker form of the full-dataset leakage present in other designs, but it still compromises the independence of validation-set hyperparameter selection.

Section 6 explicitly states: 'If the model does not perform as expected on the test data, slight parameter adjustments are permissible to ensure alignment with operational goals.' This is a direct endorsement of test-set guided tuning—the most fundamental violation of holdout data integrity. No experiment that adjusts model parameters based on test-set performance can claim a valid generalization estimate. The design frames this as 'iterative refinement,' but it renders the test-set performance figure meaningless as an unbiased estimate of future performance.

The RL formulation uses a DQN agent that selects from 10–20 candidate routes generated by 'a fast heuristic route generator.' This means the DQN's action space is constrained to routes that the heuristic would have generated—the DQN can only improve over the heuristic by ranking among heuristic-generated alternatives, not by discovering fundamentally different routes. The comparison is therefore not DQN vs. heuristic; it is DQN-constrained-to-heuristic-route-set vs. single-heuristic-choice. Any improvement is an upper-bounded, limited refinement rather than a demonstration that deep RL discovers superior route structures.

With only 12–18 months of data from a single urban operator, the training set (months 1–9) spans fewer than two full seasonal cycles, which the design itself identifies as a concern. Nine months is insufficient to represent the full seasonal demand variation that is claimed to be captured by stratification.
defender_raw:
The deep Q-learning approach to last-mile delivery routing is well-suited to the problem structure. Urban delivery routing is inherently sequential and context-dependent: each routing decision changes the traffic environment that subsequent decisions will encounter. Classical heuristics treat each delivery independently; RL can learn the inter-dependencies. The DQN architecture with discrete candidate route selection is a practical engineering choice that makes the action space tractable while preserving the ability to learn context-dependent policies.

The multi-component reward function — negative delivery time plus a customer satisfaction penalty — is correctly designed. Penalizing satisfaction drops with a coefficient of 5 (significantly higher than the per-minute time penalty) ensures the agent does not sacrifice service quality for marginal speed gains. The customer satisfaction constraint is enforced not just in training but as a hard gate in model selection (the policy is rejected if satisfaction drops below baseline), which is more rigorous than treating it as a soft optimization target.

The temporal split — 9 months training, 3 months validation, 6 months test — provides a 6-month evaluation window that captures multiple seasonal and weather patterns, making the generalization estimate robust to any single unusual month. Seasonal and geographic stratification within each split ensures representative coverage of both traffic regimes and urban zones across all evaluation periods.

Fitting transformers on combined training and validation data (months 1-12) rather than training only is a defensible decision for parameter stability in a domain with high seasonal variation. The authors explicitly acknowledge the minimal data leakage trade-off. Given that the test set remains strictly held out, the impact on evaluation validity is negligible, and the benefit for preprocessing stability in a high-variance domain is genuine.
all_issues_raised: Transformers fit on combined train+validation data—validation set distribution informs training-phase scaling, compromising independence of hyperparameter selection
Explicit test-set guided adjustment permitted in Section 6—direct violation of holdout integrity renders test performance meaningless as an unbiased estimate
DQN action space constrained to heuristic-generated routes—comparison is DQN route-ranking vs. heuristic route-selection, not a demonstration of superior route discovery
Training set covers only 9 months (<2 seasonal cycles)—insufficient for the seasonal generalization claims the design makes
Heuristic baseline has no learnable parameters and receives no tuning, while DQN undergoes episode-level training plus hyperparameter search—comparison is not architecturally equivalent
all_issues_adjudicated: Explicit test-set guided adjustment permitted in Section 6—direct violation of holdout integrity renders test performance meaningless as an unbiased estimate
DQN action space constrained to heuristic-generated routes—comparison is DQN route-ranking vs. heuristic route-selection, not a demonstration of superior route discovery
Training set covers only 9 months (<2 seasonal cycles)—insufficient for the seasonal generalization claims the design makes
